nativeRange.habitat: Marine demersal; inhabits sandy and rubble-strewn reef areas at depths of roughly 6–52 m

decorPreferences: live rock rubble for den construction

diet.notes: Generally fed a variety of meaty foods including marine fish flesh, crustacean flesh, and mysis shrimp. Has been reported to readily accept frozen preparations. Tends to feed from the entrance of its burrow, snapping at passing prey.

summary: The Chinstrap Jawfish is a recently described Indo-Pacific species recognized by its distinctive black band across the chin and pale spots along the body. Generally considered intermediate in difficulty, it requires a deep mixed substrate for burrowing and a tight-fitting lid to prevent escapes.
careNotes:
The Chinstrap Jawfish (Opistognathus wassi) was formally described in 2023 and may still appear in the trade under the older designation 'Opistognathus sp 6'. It is recognized by conspicuous large pale spots along the body, a prominent ocellus (eyespot) on the dorsal fin, and the distinctive black band extending from the lower jaw to the chin that gives it its common name.

This species tends to construct burrows in the substrate and will rearrange rubble, shells, and substrate material to fortify its den. A minimum of 7–10 cm (3–4 inches) of mixed substrate — fine sand combined with coarser rubble and shell fragments — is generally advised to allow natural burrowing behavior. Rocks and broken coral branches placed near the substrate have been noted as useful den-reinforcement material.

Jawfish are well-documented jumpers and have been known to leap from aquariums, particularly when startled or newly introduced. A tight-fitting lid is strongly recommended.

The Chinstrap Jawfish is generally peaceful with most reef inhabitants but tends to be territorial toward conspecifics. Only one specimen should be kept per tank unless a mated pair is available, and the tank should be large enough to provide distinct burrow territories if keeping a pair. It may occasionally pose a minor threat to very small ornamental shrimp.
breedingNotes: Jawfish are reported to be mouthbrooders, meaning males carry and incubate the egg mass inside their mouths until the eggs hatch. Spawning has been documented in captivity for related species in the genus, most notably the Yellowhead Jawfish. Captive breeding of the Chinstrap Jawfish specifically has not been widely documented in hobbyist literature.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name as Opistognathus wassi (Smith-Vaniz, 2023). Provided adult size (6.2 cm SL), native range countries, depth range (6–52 m), family, and order. Temperature/pH/salinity data not available on FishBase for this species — sourced from LiveAquaria only.

fish.breedingNotes: Males are reported to incubate eggs orally. Captive breeding has been documented for related jawfish species but is not widely reported for O. wassi specifically.
